draft.introduction: The parable of the Good Samaritan, unique to Luke. A lawyer asks “Who is my neighbor?” and Jesus answers with a story that makes a despised Samaritan the moral hero—a deliberate provocation.

translation.text: And behold, a lawyer stood up to put him to the test, saying, “Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?” He said to him, “What is written in the Law?” And he answered, “You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, and your neighbor as yourself.” And he said, “You have answered correctly; do this, and you will live.” But he, desiring to justify himself, said, “And who is my neighbor?” Jesus replied with a parable: A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell among robbers. A Samaritan had compassion and bound up his wounds. “Which of these three proved to be a neighbor?” He said, “The one who showed him mercy.”
translation.original_text: τίς τούτων τῶν τριῶν πλησίον δοκεῖ σοι γεγονέναι

translator_notes.note: Jesus reverses the lawyer’s question. The lawyer asked “Who is my neighbor?” (seeking to limit obligation). Jesus asks “Which proved to be a neighbor?”—transforming the question from boundary-drawing to active compassion.
